message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Aim</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This study examines the relative influence of personal resilience, social support and organisational support in reducing COVID-19 anxiety in frontline nurses.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Anxiety related to the COVID-19 pandemic is prevalent in the nursing workforce, potentially affecting nurses’ well-being and work performance. Identifying factors that could help maintain mental health and reduce coronavirus-related anxiety among frontline nurses is imperative. Currently, no studies have been conducted examining the influence of personal resilience, social support and organisational support in reducing COVID-19 anxiety among nurses.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This cross-sectional study involved 325 registered nurses from the Philippines using four standardised scales.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Of the 325 nurses in the study, 123 (37.8%) were found to have dysfunctional levels of anxiety. Using multiple linear regression analyses, social support (
                    <jats:italic>β</jats:italic>
                    = −0.142,
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    = 0.011), personal resilience (
                    <jats:italic>β</jats:italic>
                    = −0.151,
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    = 0.008) and organisational support (
                    <jats:italic>β</jats:italic>
                    = −0.127,
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    = 0.023) predicted COVID-19 anxiety. Nurse characteristics were not associated with COVID-19 anxiety.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Resilient nurses and those who perceived higher organisational and social support were more likely to report lower anxiety related to COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Implication for Nursing Management</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>COVID-19 anxiety may be addressed through organisational interventions, including increasing social support, assuring adequate organisational support, providing psychological and mental support services and providing resilience-promoting and stress management interventions.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: COVID-19 Anxiety Among Frontline Nurses: Predictive Role of Organisational Support, Personal Resilience and Social Support
